policy_statement: Review protocols for inspecting creeks and stormwater infrastructure to minimize flood risks.
mechanism_description: Reviewing protocols for inspecting creeks and stormwater infrastructure helps identify issues earlier and improves maintenance actions, reducing flood risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme rainfall) → improved stormwater system condition/performance → ↓ flood exposure/impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city updates and applies standardized inspection protocols for creeks, culverts, catch basins, and stormwater infrastructure, increasing inspection frequency/quality and triggering timely maintenance/repairs.  
2) Mediating physical process: Earlier identification and removal of obstructions, repair of damaged conveyance/structures, and correction of capacity or erosion issues improves hydraulic performance and reduces system failures/overflow during peak flows.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced flood frequency/severity and associated damages to people, property, and services under current and future precipitation extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing flood damages by lowering exposure and failure likelihood of stormwater/creek systems during extreme rainfall.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on stormwater system reliability and conveyance to reduce flood impacts from extreme rainfall.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While better maintenance can marginally improve operational efficiency, the main climate-relevant intent and effect is risk reduction from climate hazards (flooding), i.e., adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If inspections are primarily used to optimize pumping energy or reduce wastewater overflows for methane/N2O reasons, the primary could shift toward mitigation/resource efficiency—but that is not the dominant pathway described here.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy centered on inspection protocols for stormwater/creek infrastructure is fundamentally about identifying issues early to prevent/limit flooding and related damages, aligning consistently with adaptation.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Any secondary subtype (e.g., “infrastructure resilience,” “early warning/monitoring,” “asset management”) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be co-beneficial for water quality and, in combined sewer contexts, could reduce overflow-related emissions; however, unless explicitly framed/quantified around GHG reductions, the dominant pathway remains flood risk reduction (adaptation).
key_indicators: "Review the protocols"; "inspecting creeks and stormwater infrastructure"; "to minimize flood risks"
co_benefits: Improved water quality (less debris/sediment), reduced erosion and habitat disturbance, better service reliability/asset lifespan
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a performance-standard if it specified required inspection frequencies/levels of service; as written it is a process/protocol review, so classified as planning-strategy (enabling).
